title: Jack Macgougan

infobox.value: **Chair of the [Northern Ireland Labour Party](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Labour_Party "Northern Ireland Labour Party")**  1945–1946 | Succeeded by John Boyle
**President of the [Irish Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Irish_Trades_Union_Congress "Irish Trades Union Congress")**  1958 | Succeeded by Walter Carpenter

infobox.value: **General Secretary of the [National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Tailors_and_Garment_Workers "National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers")**  1969–1979 | Succeeded by [Alec Smith](/wiki/Alec_Smith_(trade_unionist) "Alec Smith (trade unionist)")

(21 August 1913 – 12 December 1998) was a [labour](/wiki/Trade_unionist "Trade unionist"), [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") and [Irish republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") activist in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), and a President of the [Irish Congress of Trade Unions](/wiki/Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions "Irish Congress of Trade Unions").

Byrne, Patrick (1994). [*The Republican Congress Revisited (with a foreword by Nora Harkin)*](https://www.leftarchive.ie/workspace/documents/22057-ir-rep-cong-94.pdf) (PDF). Dublin: Connolly association Pamphlet. pp. 5, 15. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0952231700](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0952231700 "Special:BookSources/0952231700").
Tallon, Ruth (2016). [*Winnifred and George*](https://www.visitwestbelfast.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/07/winnie-and-george-low-res.pdf) (PDF). Belfast: Failte Feirste Thiar. p. 10.
"Jack Macgougan", *[The Irish Times](/wiki/The_Irish_Times "The Irish Times")*, 3 May 1999
Andrew Finlay, *Governing Ethnic Conflict: Consociationism, Identity and the Price of Peace*, p.93
"[Discussion between Richard (Dick) Montague and Ciaran Crossey](http://www.workersrepublic.org/Pages/Ireland/Trotskyism/montague.html), Arguments for a Workers Republic

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Jack Macgougan** (21 August 1913 – 12 December 1998) was a [labour](/wiki/Trade_unionist "Trade unionist"), [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") and [Irish republican](/wiki/Irish_republicanism "Irish republicanism") activist in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast"), [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), and a President of the [Irish Congress of Trade Unions](/wiki/Irish_Congress_of_Trade_Unions "Irish Congress of Trade Unions").

Born in [Belfast](/wiki/Belfast "Belfast") to a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") family, Macgougan became an active trade unionist at an early age. In 1935 he was elected Secretary of the [Socialist Party of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_Northern_Ireland "Socialist Party of Northern Ireland"), a [Northern Ireland Labour Party](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Labour_Party "Northern Ireland Labour Party")-affiliate split from the [Independent Labour Party](/wiki/Independent_Labour_Party "Independent Labour Party") (NILP).[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

In 1934, along with [Victor Halley](/wiki/Victor_Halley "Victor Halley"), [Jack White](/wiki/Jack_White_(Irish_socialist) "Jack White (Irish socialist)") and other northern trade unionists and socialists, he attended the convention in [Athlone](/wiki/Athlone "Athlone") that established the broad "anti-imperialist" [Republican Congress](/wiki/Republican_Congress "Republican Congress"), an initiative of a left split from the [Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Irish_Republican_Army_(1922%E2%80%931969) "Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) From 1936 he was active, alongside [Betty Sinclair](/wiki/Betty_Sinclair "Betty Sinclair"), [Winifred Carney](/wiki/Winifred_Carney "Winifred Carney"), Halley and others, in organising relief aid for the [Spanish Republic](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") in [civil war](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") with [Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco").[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

In the [1938 Northern Ireland general election](/wiki/1938_Northern_Ireland_general_election "1938 Northern Ireland general election"), he stood for the NILP in [Belfast Oldpark](/wiki/Belfast_Oldpark_(Northern_Ireland_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast Oldpark (Northern Ireland Parliament constituency)"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) taking second place, with 40.8% of the vote.[[6]](#cite_note-belfast-6) In 1945, he was appointed Irish Regional Organiser of the [National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers](/wiki/National_Union_of_Tailors_and_Garment_Workers "National Union of Tailors and Garment Workers") (NUTGW).[[7]](#cite_note-saothar-7)

Macgougan was Chair of the NILP in 1945–1946,[[8]](#cite_note-8) but became unhappy with its increasingly [unionist](/wiki/Ulster_unionism "Ulster unionism") stance. Along with Halley and [Harry Diamond](/wiki/Harry_Diamond_(politician) "Harry Diamond (politician)") he supported the establishment of the [Irish Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Ireland) "Labour Party (Ireland)") in Northern Ireland.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1949, he was elected for the party to [Belfast City Council](/wiki/Belfast_City_Council "Belfast City Council").[[7]](#cite_note-saothar-7) He later stood unsuccessfully for the party in [South Down](/wiki/South_Down_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "South Down (UK Parliament constituency)") at the [1950 general election](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election")[[10]](#cite_note-10) (the [Anti-Partition League](/wiki/Irish_Anti-Partition_League "Irish Anti-Partition League") decided not to oppose him, but priests denounced him as a communist)[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) and [Belfast Falls](/wiki/Belfast_Falls_(Northern_Ireland_Parliament_constituency) "Belfast Falls (Northern Ireland Parliament constituency)") at the [1953 Northern Ireland general election](/wiki/1953_Northern_Ireland_general_election "1953 Northern Ireland general election").[[6]](#cite_note-belfast-6) He lost his council seat in 1958. That year, he served as President of the [Irish Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Irish_Trades_Union_Congress "Irish Trades Union Congress") and, in 1965, he became President of its merger with the [Congress of Irish Unions](/wiki/Congress_of_Irish_Unions "Congress of Irish Unions"), the [Irish Congress of Trades Unions](/wiki/Irish_Congress_of_Trades_Unions "Irish Congress of Trades Unions"). In 1969, he became General Secretary of the UK-wide NUTGW, and also served on the General Council of the British [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress").[[7]](#cite_note-saothar-7)

In 1948, along with Halley and the writer and Anti-Partition League speaker [Denis Ireland](/wiki/Denis_Ireland "Denis Ireland"), MacGougan Ireland was member of the Belfast [1798](/wiki/Irish_Rebellion_of_1798 "Irish Rebellion of 1798") Commemoration Commiitee. After being denied access to the city centre, they rallied 30,000 in Corrigan Park in nationalist west Belfast.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) MacGougan reminded the crowd that the [United Irish](/wiki/Society_of_United_Irishmen "Society of United Irishmen") leader "[Wolfe Tone](/wiki/Wolfe_Tone "Wolfe Tone") was an advocate of the new social forces that arose in all parts of the world" and that when they paid tribute to the United Irishmen they were to remember that "they had the closest fraternal links with the democratic forces in other countries".[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

MacGougan retired to [Milton Keynes](/wiki/Milton_Keynes "Milton Keynes") in England where he died on 14 December 1998.
